title: 第 48 節
en: 348 ASSIMILATION, J. SCHMIDT. [chap.

In conclusion Schmidt gives some cases where a vowel seems to have been influenced by a preceding vowel, but these cases are, as he admits himself, very doubtful. Nor can we look on any one even of Schmidt's surer observations as more than phonetic tendencies which require to be justified in every single case. The minute conditions to which every one of them is liable, show by themselves how many undercurrents there are which may either favour or counteract such tendencies. Still by an accumulation of instances and counter-instances Schmidt and Noreen have widened our view, and shown how many circumstances have to be taken into account before we can declare positively that the presence of a vowel such as a, e, o, either confirms or forbids the identification of words in Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin.
Consonants.

It has often been asked whether, for etymological purposes, vowels are more important or consonants. Such a question is really foolish. At first sight the consonantal skeleton of a word may seem more important, and, as prima facie evidence, consonants are no doubt more helpful than vowels. When we see Latin pater and Sk. pitar, we are at once impressed by their consonantal similarity, and we feel inclined to waive any dissimilarity of vowels. Still, unless other cases could be produced in which a Latin a is represented by Sk. i, there are scholars who would insist that the two words pater and pitar, in spite of the identity of their meaning and in spite of the identity of their consonantal outline, v] CONSONANTS. 349 could not possibly be traced back to the same source. This seems to be going too far. Anyhow, it should be considered whether it was likely that the idea of father should have been expressed by words derived by the same suffix from two distinct roots, varying from each other in the colour of one of their vowels only. In the days preceding the discovery of Verner's Law, it would have been much more justifiable, as I pointed out before, to doubt the etymological relationship of Gothic fadar and Latin pater, on account of the absence of the aspiration of the dental, as required by Grimm's Law ; but I do not recollect any such protest being raised against it, common sense in this case, as in others, fortunately proving stronger than respect for phonetic laws. In the course of a long life I have myself been blamed and ridiculed sometimes for too blind an observance of phonetic laws. I well remember how men of considerable literary eminence represented it as the height of scholastic pedantry to deny any relationship between such words as KaKelv and to call, cura and care, Dame in Dame Dieu and Damn ! Nor have I on the other side escaped severe blame for having ventured, in defiance of all phonetic restrictions, to connect Sk. deva and Oeos, Sk. Varmia and Ovpavos, Sk. Vn'tra and on argument. If better etymologies can be suggested for these words than those I have brought forward, there would at once be an end of all controversy, and no one would be better pleased than myself. But though I am well aware of the irregularities involved in these identifications of Sanskrit and Greek words, I have never despaired 35° CONSONANTS. [chap.
of being able to justify them both in their vowels and in their consonants.

The discoveries in the realm of consonants have certainly been quite as important as those in the realm of vowels. In the same way, for instance, as it was found that the Sanskrit alphabet had but one representative for the three shades of the simple vowel, a, e, o, it was found that several of its consonants also possessed more than one power, represented more than one definite sound. Here also the facts themselves were well known to the ancient grammarians of India. They taught, for instance, that some roots ending in 5r g and f h change their finals into ^ and ^, while others change them into Unguals.
Two kinds of Palatals.

Thus spr^ yu<7 forms the participle *pfj: yukta/i ; <H duh forms ^Ttf: (i. e. dugh + tah) ; but *f5T mrig forms Jre: mrisht&h ; f^rf lib forms ir\%: lidha.h. Every tiro knew these facts, or had to learn them from his Sanskrit Grammar (M. M., §§ 119, 124, 127, 129). The same applies to roots in ^ s. Though we cannot test this ^ s in the past participle, because here both f^^ dis would form f^: disht&h and f%"^ vis, f^j: vishteA, yet in the nom. sing, f^^ dis appears as f^ejj dik, but f%^ vis as f$z vitf. Therefore here also the ^ s represents two distinct powers. But though these facts were perfectly well known, it was Ascoli who first drew the subtle though important conclusion that when the effects are different, the causes also must be different, or, in other words, that the letters ^ s, *r g, and f h, must represent in Sanskrit two powers, the one guttural, v] TWO KINDS OF PALATALS. 35 1 the other non-guttural, and that they may therefore in cognate languages also be expected to have different representatives. It should be remembered, however, that like other phonetic observations this also is not without exceptions. These exceptions were clearly indicated by Pa/iini, and have to be accepted and taken into account, even though they cannot be accounted for. There are roots in Sanskrit (Paw. VIII, 2, 33) the final of which is treated both as guttural and as lingual. This applies to such roots as ^f druh, g^ muh, ^rf snuh, f%^ snih. We find, therefore, both y^j dhruk and i*Z dhru^, both $*t*t: drugdha/i and j^s: drudhah, jpej: mugdha/i and jj<j: miidh&h. We find likewise from ^^ y&g both ■^jf^ejj n'tvik and ^%z deve£ ; from »p^ nas both «f^ nak and «rc na£. Again, there are roots in Sanskrit the final of which is treated both as guttural and as dental. These are mentioned by Pacini (VIII, 2, 34). They are «Tf nah and ^ ah. Language here, as elsewhere, asserts its dialectic freedom against rules and analogies. We look in vain for a reason, whether phonetic or otherwise, and must learn to accept facts such as they are, even though we are unable to account for them. As these so-called irregularities are not confined in their effects to the derivatives of these roots in Sanskrit, it follows that they must be referred to the ProtoAryan period.

What is important, however, is that we shall have to admit in Sanskrit, as well as in other Aryan languages, the existence of a class of consonants which, in order to distinguish them from the ordinary palatals, the % ^, ^T, ij, ^, and ^, h, kh, g, gh, ft, and s, we may provisionally call Linguo-palatals, 352 TWO KINDS OF PALATALS. [chap.

namely the 5T g, when representing z, and the f h, when representing zh, and the ^ s, when representing z t. Thus we find corresponding to Sk. satam, Lit. szhhtas, with assibilated s, but Gr. eKarov, Lat. centum, Celt, cet, with guttural tenuis. Likewise Sk. g\vn% where g stands for z, which appears as z in Lit. zirnis, Slav, zrino, while Lat. has the guttural media in granum, and Goth, the corresponding tenuis kaurn. Likewise the Sk. h, when it represents an original zh, is represented in Sanskrit by the h in hima, Avestic zima, and therefore by z in Lit. zema, Slav, zima, but in Greek by Xeijuw, in Lat. by hiems, in Celt, by gam. See also Sk. sroms, Lit. szlaunes, Lett, slauna, but Lat. clunis, 0. N. hlaun.
Two kinds of Gutturals.

In the same way it was discovered that what were called in Sanskrit the gutturals (ka^Aya) represented really two classes of letters, some of them being liable to labialisation (and in Greek to dentalisation), while others were not. The fact that the Sanskrit gutturals k, kh, g, gh are liable to be represented in Greek, Latin, Celtic, and German by labials and dentals, was known from the earliest days of Comparative Philology, for who would have doubted that Sk. kas was Gr. /cos and ttos, Lat. quis, Cymr. pwy, Goth, hvas, or that Gr. ri corresponded to Lat. quid, Sk. Hd ? No one ever doubted that Sk. pan&a was Gr. irivre, Lat. quinque, Cymr. pimp, Goth, fimf, or again that Sk. Aatvar was Lat. quatuor, Gr. reWape?, Goth, fidvor. But what was even more curious was that the same languages, viz. Sanskrit, Zend, Armenian, Lituanian, and v] TWO KINDS OF GUTTURALS. 353 Slavonic, which, as we saw, possessed the assibilated Linguo-palatals in cases where the other languages, viz. Greek, Italian, and German, presented unassibilated gutturals, resisted also the labialisation or dentalisation of gutturals in cases where the other class of languages allowed it. Thus we find that Sk. Aa was Lat. que, Cymric -p, Gothic -h, and Gr. re. But the conditions under which these apparently violent modifications took place were not known, and the merit of having gradually discovered some of them belongs again, I think, to Ascoli, though in conjunction with other scholars, such as Fick, Schmidt, and Brugmann. It was not known before, that there were two classes of languages, one which never labialised or dentalised, and another which never assibilated ; nor was it clearly perceived that even the labialising languages do not always labialise their gutturals, and that in this case the fate of the unlabialised gutturals becomes much the same as the fate of the palatals.

Much ingenuity has been spent on the solution of this phonetic problem, and I believe its solution would have become much easier if letters had not been treated here also, as in the case of the Lautverschiebung, as things existing by themselves that can be changed from one language to another. Even the Greek dialects cannot be treated in that way. There is no evidence to show, for instance, that in words like satam, a hundred, and the Greek zkcltov, s ever became k, or k became s. We cannot even realise such an idea, nay, it seems to me to involve a contradiction in itself. We have in the end always to deal with human beings, some of whom, so long as we know, pronounced satam, while vol. 1. A a 354 TW0 KINDS OF GUTTUKALS. [chap.

others pronounced katon. One speaker had as much right as another, nor could it be decided by a majority of speakers whether s was more original than k, or k than s. To say that Sk. s becomes Greek k, or vice versa, seems to me utterly unmeaning, unless we accept it as a mere mode of metaphorical expression, which is more expeditious, and need in that sense not be objected to. Why in the same word the Hindu pronounced s, and the Greek k, is a question that cannot be answered, nay, that we have really no right to ask. Who could say whether the q of quinque and the c of Ir. coic were originally p as in irevre (Proto-Aryan penqe) 1, or whether the initial p was original, and the q a modification of it ? A change of p into q is so exceptional that we should hesitate to admit it except in languages which have a decided gutturalising tendency, such as Irish, which changes even pascha into caisc 2.

True, certain concomitant circumstances have been discovered by Fick, Ascoli, and others, which seem to favour or to prevent assibilation and labialisation, but that is very different from saying that we are able to account for such changes. It is very different when, as in Italian, we can say that there is an almost mechanical necessity for our pronouncing c before i or e as ch. Here we have a clear case of Nacheinander, of successive change, though even here the first start may be traced back as far as Umbrian. But in the difference between Sk. s and Gr. k, between Sk. p. and Lat. q, we have clearly a case of Nebeneinander, of parallel dialectic 1 Brugmann, Grundriss, § 321.
2 J. Bhys, Lectures on Welsh Philology, p. 373.

v] TWO KINDS OF GUTTURALS. 355 modification, which is due to phonetic idiosyncrasies going back to a period previous to the Aryan Separation, and that cannot be further accounted for. The same applies to the labialising and nonlabialising of the gutturals. Both have an equal right, and the causes which seem to favour or prevent the process of labialising are only assistant, but not efficient causes. These changes are neither dynamic nor purely mechanical, they are individual, and no more to be accounted for than the dh of dha and the d of da. They are quite different also from changes due to certain predilections peculiar to one or the other of the Aryan languages in their separate existence, as, for instance, we may truly say that Latin has a predilection for qu, and therefore does not labialise a guttural if it can help it.

Thus, if we take the guttural or palatal of hi, we find the labial in iroivrj, and the dental in oltto-tlo-ls, corresponding to Sk. hiti, &ayate, Avest. ka§na. Here Latin would not use p for the initial guttural, and if poena belongs really to this family of words, we shall have to accept it as borrowed from Greek, unless indeed we derive it from a totally different root, the same which in Latin gives us purus, punio, impune, &c. 1 These phonetic tendencies and idiosyncrasies cannot be used with any amount of safety for historical purposes, except with great caution. If Bopp concluded that the speakers of the assibilating languages must have separated from the parent stem at a later time than the other European languages, he went too far, while Schleicher hardly went far enough 1 See Chips, vol. iii, p. 193. A a 2 356 TWO KINDS OF GUTTURALS. [chap.

when he denied to this fact any historical significance at all, and ascribed it to mere accident. The whole idea of a uniform original language, free as yet from all dialectic peculiarities, is, as I have often tried to show, a mere postulate, unsupported by any evidence, or even by any real analogy. As long as we know anything of any language we know it in a state of dialectic fermentation, and the germs of this dialectic variety as between the great branches of Aryan speech can only be referred to what is called the Pan-Aryan period. That the idea of a pedigree of the Aryan languages is self-contradictory, I think I showed as far back as 1872 \ There are dialectic changes which are clearly successive, as when carus becomes cher, or hafoc becomes hawk. But these changes belong to a later and altogether different phase ; they are mechanical and are produced with almost mechanical precision, while the old dialectic varieties as between Sanskrit, Greek, Latin and German (particularly the changes included under the name of Lautverschiebung), cannot be accounted for as successive mechanical changes or so-called corruptions, but have to be accepted as we accept other facts of language, as collateral varieties and as purely spontaneous. It is Ascoli's and Fick's great merit to have accounted for some of these variations, and their observations possess therefore great practical usefulness. They help us to distinguish between the fates of what used to be called gutturals and palatals, and to distinguish again among gutturals between those that are liable to labialisation and those that are not ; but more than that they cannot do.
1 Strassburg Lecture, see Chips, vol. iii, p. 174.

v] TWO KINDS OF GUTTURALS. 357 We know now that there is a whole class of languages which never labialise or dentalise, and that in certain words even the labialising languages abstain from labialising their gutturals. The rules to be observed are therefore that in the assibilating languages, the linguo-palatals appear in —
Sk. Avestic. Lit. Slav.
as ^ s (or \sh) s, s s (sz) s as ^g (for z) z, z z z as f h (for zh) z z z but in the non-assibilating languages —
Gk. Lat. Celtic. Ger.
asx c c k, x, g, 7 as y g g k as x h, g S S> y In the non-assibilating but labialising languages, the true gutturals appear in —
Gk. Lat. Ir. Brit. Ger. iinSk., Zend, as k, it, r (a) (not p) c\ qu c p xw2, w, x, JLit., & Slav.
y, f , 15 (as gutturals as y, 0, 8 (C) g, gu, v g, b b, g k, kw, p as x, 0, 0 gu, v, b, f g, b b, g w, y, (b)

The gutturals liable to labialisation are sometimes written and printed q, g, gh. This might be useful if generally adopted, but the difficulty is to get the types when they are wanted. In words where the non-assibilating (but labialising) languages do not labialise their gutturals, these gutturals have the same fate as the gutturals and what we call the palatals in the assibilating languages. —
Sk. Gk. Lat. Ir. Brit. Ger. Lit. Slav.
cji k, "*{ s, \Jc k c c c k, x, g, 7 k k, c, c 1 g, %9 7 g g g k g g' d2> dz (*§ kty ^ gh x b, g g g g, y g g, dz, dz 1 Brugmann, i, p. 323, note 4 ; as to b = g, see p. 324.
zh: 同化，J‧施密特。[章

最後，施密特舉出若干案例，其中一個母音似乎受到前面母音的影響，但這些案例，如他本人所承認的，都極為可疑。我們也不能將施密特任何較為確實的觀察視為不過是音韻傾向，而這些傾向在每一個單獨案例中都需要得到論證。這些傾向所受的細微條件本身就表明，有多少暗流可能既有利於要麼抵消此類傾向。儘管如此，通過大量正反例證的積累，施密特與諾瑞恩已經拓寬了我們的視野，並展示了在我們能夠明確宣稱梵文、希臘文和拉丁文中某一母音（如 a、e、o）的出現究竟是確認還是禁止詞彙辨認之前，必須考慮到多少種情況。
輔音。

關於詞源學的目的而言，母音是否比輔音更重要，這個問題經常被提出。但這種問題實在是愚蠢的。乍看之下，一個詞的輔音骨架似乎更為重要，而且作為初步的證據，輔音確實比母音更有幫助。當我們看到拉丁文的 pater 和梵文的 pitar 時，我們立刻被它們的輔音相似性所打動，因而傾向於忽視母音的任何差異。然而，除非還能舉出其他例子，說明拉丁文的 a 在梵文中被 i 所代表，否則有些學者會堅持認為，pater 和 pitar 這兩個詞，儘管它們的意義相同，儘管它們的輔音輪廓也相同，卻不可能被追溯到同一個來源。這似乎過於牽強。無論如何，應該考慮的是，用衍生自兩個不同根源、僅在母音色澤上相差一個的詞來表達「父親」這一概念的做法，是否有可能。在維爾納定律被發現之前的年代裡，正如我之前所指出的那樣，因為牙音的氣音缺失（這是格林姆定律所要求的），而懷疑哥德語 fadar 和拉丁文 pater 之間的詞源關係，會更加合理。但我不記得當時曾有人對此提出任何這樣的異議，在這個例子中，常識幸運地證明比對音韻法則的尊重更為強大。在我漫長的人生中，我自己有時因為過於盲目地遵守音韻法則而受到責備和嘲笑。我至今還清楚地記得，一些相當有文學聲望的人如何將否認 kakείv 和 to call、cura 和 care、Dame 與 Dame Dieu 及 Damn 之間存在任何關係的行為，表現為學究氣十足的頂點！另一方面，我也曾因為敢於違抗所有音韻限制，將梵文的 deva 和 Θεός、梵文的 Varmia 和 Οὐρανός、梵文的 Vṛtra 和某個論證相聯繫而遭受嚴厲責難。如果能為這些詞提出比我所提出的更好的詞源學解釋，一切爭論便會立刻煙消雲散，而我將是最感高興的人。儘管我深知這些梵文和希臘文詞彙的識別涉及不規則性，但我從未對其失去信心。
在其元音和輔音兩方面都能夠為它們進行正當化。
在輔音領域的發現確實與在母音領域的發現一樣重要。例如，就如同人們發現梵文字母對於簡單母音 a、e、o 的三種音色只有一個代表符號一樣，人們也發現其若干輔音同樣擁有不止一種音值，代表不止一個確定的聲音。在這方面，事實本身對於古代印度的文法學家而言也是眾所周知的。例如，他們教導說，某些以 g 和 h 結尾的詞根將其詞尾變為某些聲音，而其他詞根則將其變為軟顎音。
兩種腭音

因此，*yuj* 形成分詞 *yukta/i*；*duh* 形成 *dughta/h*（即 dugh + tah）；但 *mrig* 形成 *mrishta/h*；*lib* 形成 *lidha/h*。每一位初學者都知道這些事實，或是必須從他的梵文文法中學習這些事實（M. M., §§ 119、124、127、129）。同樣的情況也適用於以 *s* 結尾的詞根。雖然我們無法在完成式分詞中檢驗這個 *s*，因為在這裡 *dis* 和 *disht/a/h* 都會形成，*vis* 和 *vishta/h* 亦然，但在主格單數中，*dis* 出現為 *dik*，而 *vis* 則出現為 *vit*。因此這裡的 *s* 也代表兩種不同的力量。然而儘管這些事實早已為人所知，但首先得出這一微妙卻重要結論的是阿斯科利（Ascoli），他指出當效果不同時，原因也必然不同；換句話說，梵文中的字母 *s*、*g* 和 *h* 必須代表兩種力量，其中一種是喉音的，另一種是非喉音的，因此在相關的語言中也可以預期它們具有不同的代表。然而應當記住，就像其他語音觀察一樣，這個結論也並非沒有例外。帕你尼（Pa/n/ini）曾明確指出這些例外，必須被接納並納入考慮，儘管無法對其作出解釋。梵文中有一些詞根（Paw. VIII, 2, 33），其詞尾既被視為喉音又被視為舌音。這適用於諸如 *druh*、*muh*、*snuh*、*snih* 等詞根。因此我們發現既有 *dhruk* 也有 *dhru/s*，既有 *drugdha/h* 也有 *drudhah*，既有 *mugdha/h* 也有 *mudh/ah*。我們同樣發現從 *yag* 既有 *n'tvík* 也有 *devé/s*；從 *nas* 既有 *nak* 也有 *na/s*。再者，梵文中有一些詞根的詞尾既被視為喉音又被視為牙音。帕你尼提及過這些詞根（VIII, 2, 34）。它們是 *nah* 和 *ah*。語言在此如同其他地方一樣，維護著其相對於規則和類比的辯證自由。我們徒勞地尋求理由，無論是語音上的還是其他方面的，必須學會接納事實本身，儘管我們無法對其作出解釋。既然這些所謂的不規則現象在這些詞根衍生物上的影響並不僅限於梵文，它們就必須被追溯到原始雅利安時期。
然而，重要的是，我們必須在梵語以及其他雅利安語言中承認存在著一類輔音，為了將它們與普通的硬顎音—— च्(ch)、छ्(chh)、ज्(j)、झ्(jh)、ञ्(ñ)、और क्(k)、ख्(kh)、ग्(g)、घ्(gh)、ट्(ṭ)、और स्(s)——區別開來，我們可以暫時將其稱為舌硬顎音（linguo-palatals）。352 兩類硬顎音。[第一章

亦即特尤斯（5T g），當代表 z 時，以及 f h，當代表 zh 時，以及 ^ s，當代表 z t 時。因此我們發現對應於梵文 satam，立陶宛文 szhhtas，具有咝音化的 s，但希臘文 eKarov，拉丁文 centum，凱爾特文 cet，具有軟腭清音。同樣地，梵文 g\vn%，其中 g 代表 z，在立陶宛文中表現為 z in zirnis，斯拉夫文 zrino，而拉丁文具有軟腭濁音 granum，哥德文則具有相應的清音 kaurn。同樣地，梵文 h，當它代表原始的 zh 時，在梵文中由 h 在 hima 中表示，阿維斯陀文 zima，因此在立陶宛文中由 z in zema 表示，斯拉夫文 zima，但在希臘文中由 Xeijuw，在拉丁文中由 hiems，在凱爾特文中由 gam 表示。亦見梵文 sroms，立陶宛文 szlaunes，萊蒂文 slauna，但拉丁文 clunis，古北歐文 hlaun。
兩種咽音

同樣的方式也發現了，梵文中所謂的軟顎音（ka^Aya）實際上代表了兩類字母，其中某些字母容易發生唇音化（在古希臘語中則為齒音化），而其他則否。梵文軟顎音 k、kh、g、gh 在古希臘語、拉丁語、凱爾特語和日耳曼語中容易被唇音和齒音所代表的事實，從比較語言學最早的年代起就為人所知，因為誰會懷疑梵文 kas 就是古希臘語 cos 和 ttos、拉丁語 quis、威爾斯語 pwy、哥德語 hvas，或者古希臘語 ri 對應於拉丁語 quid、梵文 Hd 呢？沒有人會懷疑梵文 pañça 就是古希臘語 πέντε、拉丁語 quinque、威爾斯語 pimp、哥德語 fimf，或者梵文 çatvar 就是拉丁語 quatuor、古希臘語 τέσσαρες、哥德語 fidvor。但更為奇特的是，同樣的那些語言，即梵文、阿維斯陀語、亞美尼亞語、立陶宛語和斯拉夫語，如我們之前所見，在其他語言（即古希臘語、義大利語和日耳曼語）呈現未同音化的軟顎音之處，具有同音化的舌面軟顎音，同時在其他一類語言允許軟顎音唇音化或齒音化之處，卻抵抗這種唇音化或齒音化。因此我們發現梵文 ça 就是拉丁語 que、威爾斯語 -p、哥德語 -h 和古希臘語 τε。但這些表面上激烈的變化所發生的條件當時是不為人知的，而逐漸發現其中某些條件的功勞，我認為應該歸於阿斯科利（Ascoli），儘管還要與其他學者如菲克（Fick）、施密特（Schmidt）和布魯格曼（Brugmann）聯合。此前人們並未知曉存在著兩類語言，一類從不進行唇音化或齒音化，另一類從不進行同音化；也未曾清晰地認識到，即便是進行唇音化的語言也並非總是對其軟顎音進行唇音化，在這種情況下，未經唇音化的軟顎音的命運變得與舌面軟顎音的命運幾乎相同。

許多巧思已經用於解決這個音位問題，而我相信，如果字母在此處也不像在 Lautverschiebung（音位轉移）的情況中那樣，被當作可以從一種語言改變為另一種語言的自存之物，那麼問題的解決將會變得容易得多。即使是希臘方言也不能以那種方式處理。例如，沒有任何證據表明，在 satam（一百）和希臘文 ἑκατόν (hekatón) 這樣的詞中，s 曾經變成 k，或 k 變成 s。我們甚至無法理解這樣的觀點，更有甚者，在我看來，這似乎涉及一個自身的矛盾。最終我們總是要處理人類，其中一些人，據我們所知，說的是 satam，而

有人發音為 katon。每位說話者的權利相等，也無法由多數說話者的投票決定 s 是否比 k 更原始，或 k 是否比 s 更原始。說梵文的 s 變成希臘文的 k，或反之亦然，在我看來毫無意義，除非我們將其視為純粹的隱喻表達方式，這樣做更為簡便，從這個意義上說無須反對。為什麼在同一個詞中印度人發音為 s，而希臘人發音為 k，這個問題是無法回答的，不，我們實在沒有權利提出這個問題。誰能說 quinque 的 q 和愛爾蘭語 coic 的 c 最初是否為 p，如同 irevre（原始雅利安語 penqe）中的情況一樣¹？或者說初始的 p 是原始的，而 q 是它的變化形式？p 變成 q 的變化非常罕見，我們應該猶豫是否接納它，除非在具有明顯咽化傾向的語言中，例如愛爾蘭語，它甚至把 pascha 變成 caisc²。

誠然，菲克（Fick）、阿斯科利（Ascoli）及其他學者已發現某些伴隨的情況，似乎有利於或阻礙清擦音化（assibilation）及唇音化（labialisation），但這與我們能夠解釋這類變化迥然不同。當如同在義大利語中，我們可以說在 i 或 e 之前發音 c 為 ch 具有幾乎是機械的必然性時，情況就大不相同。這裡我們有一個清晰的後起變（Nacheinander）案例，即連續的變化，雖然即使在此，最初的起源也可以追溯至翁布里亞語（Umbrian）。但在梵文 s 與希臘文 k 之間的差異、梵文 p 與拉丁文 q 之間的差異中，我們清楚地看到的是並列變（Nebeneinander）的案例，即平行的方言變化。¹ ¹ Brugmann, *Grundriss*, § 321.
2 J. Bhys,《威爾斯語言學講座》，第373頁。

修飾現象源自於雅利安分離之前的時期中固有的音韻特殊性，這些現象無法進一步解釋。同樣的情況也適用於咽頭音的唇音化與非唇音化。兩者具有同等的權利，而似乎有利於或阻止唇音化過程的原因僅是助因，而非決定因。這些變化既非動態的，也非純粹機械的，而是個別的，與 dha 中的 dh 和 da 中的 d 一樣無法進一步說明。它們也完全不同於源自雅利安諸語言在各自獨立存在期間所特有的某些偏好而產生的變化；例如，我們可以確實地說拉丁語偏好 qu，因此盡可能不對咽頭音進行唇音化。

因此，如果我們取 hi 的咽頭音或硬顎音，我們會在 ἱροιvrj 中發現唇音，在 oltto-tlo-ls 中發現牙音，對應梵文 hiti、śayate，以及阿維斯陀語 ka§na。拉丁語在詞首的咽頭音處不會使用 p，如果 poena 真的屬於這類詞彙的族系，我們就必須接受它是從希臘語借入的說法，除非我們確實將其衍生自完全不同的詞根——同一詞根在拉丁語中給出 purus、punio、impune 等詞。¹ 這些音韻傾向性和特殊性除非謹慎小心，否則不能以任何程度的安全性用於歷史研究之目的。如果博普得出結論謂使用同位化語言的民族必定在比其他歐洲語言更晚的時間從母語分離出去，他則走得太遠了；而施萊歇爾則幾乎沒有走得夠遠¹ ¹ 參見《片簡集》第三卷，第 193 頁。

當他否認這一事實具有任何歷史重要性，而將之歸咎於純粹偶然時。整個關於統一的原始語言的觀念——該語言迄今為止不受任何方言特殊性的影響——誠如我多次試圖所示，不過是一項單純的假設，缺乏任何證據的支持，甚至缺乏任何真實的類比。只要我們對任何語言有所認識，我們就會發現它處於方言發酵的狀態，而雅利安語族各大分支之間這種方言變異的萌芽，只能被追溯至所謂的泛雅利安時期。我認為早在1872年我已經證明了雅利安語言譜系的觀念是自相矛盾的。誠然有些方言變化是明確的連續性的，例如carus變為cher，或hafoc變為hawk。但這些變化屬於一個較晚的、完全不同的階段；它們是機械性的，且幾乎以機械般的精確性而產生，而梵語、希臘語、拉丁語和日耳曼語之間的舊方言變異（特別是在音位轉換（Lautverschiebung）的名義下包括的變化），則不能被說明為連續的機械性變化或所謂的語言腐化，而是必須像我們接納語言的其他事實一樣加以接納，作為側系變異和純粹自發性的現象。這正是阿斯科利（Ascoli）和菲克（Fick）的偉大功績，他們說明了其中一些變異，因此他們的觀察具有很大的實踐效用。它們幫助我們區分所謂古喉音和齶音的命運，並進一步在古喉音之中區分那些易於唇音化的和那些不易於唇音化的；但除此之外，它們就無能為力了。
斯特拉斯堡講座，見《片段集》第三卷，第一七四頁。
**五】 兩種喉音。357** 我們現在知道，有整個一類語言從不進行唇音化或齒音化，而在某些詞彙中，即使是進行唇音化的語言也會避免對其喉音進行唇音化。因此應當遵守的規則是：在同音化（assibilating）語言中，舌‧顎音出現在——
梵文、阿維斯陀文、立陶宛文、斯拉夫文
